topic: Unit 5: A2 Themes (Young People in Society; Our Place in a Changing World)
subject: french

items.why: Students default to à. The rule: EN for feminine countries (most ending -e: France, Italie, Espagne); AU for masculine (au Portugal, au Canada); AUX for plural (aux États-Unis). À is for cities (à Paris).

items.q: Which letter is typically SILENT at the end of French words like 'petit' or 'froid'?
items.why: Students try to pronounce every letter. Final consonants in French are usually silent (CaReFuL exceptions: c, r, f, l). Petit is pronounced 'puh-TEE', not 'puh-TEET'.

items.q: What is the most common informal way to form a yes/no question in French?
items.correct: Rising intonation (statement said as a question) or using 'est-ce que'.
items.why: Students learn formal inversion first. In conversation, French uses intonation or est-ce que far more often. Inversion sounds formal/written - 'Tu parles français?' is the everyday form.

items.q: In which year did Algeria gain independence from France?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1962. “1954” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which is NOT part of the Maghreb?
items.why: Correct answer: Sénégal. “Algérie” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was Quebec's Law 101 (Charter of the French Language) passed?
items.why: The real figure is 1977. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
